title: NP Primary Care Emergency Study Set

flashcards.front: An adult with diabetes has new chest pressure and diaphoresis. What is the safest first clinical priority?
flashcards.back: Treat as possible acute coronary syndrome: assess stability, obtain urgent evaluation, and escalate care.
flashcards.rationale: Diabetes can alter symptom presentation; chest pressure with autonomic symptoms requires urgent cardiac evaluation.
flashcards.pearl: Primary care triage starts by ruling out time-sensitive threats.

flashcards.front: Which headache feature requires urgent evaluation?
flashcards.back: Thunderclap onset, neurologic deficit, fever/meningismus, pregnancy/postpartum state, cancer, immunosuppression, or worst headache of life.
flashcards.rationale: Red flags raise concern for secondary headache requiring urgent workup.
flashcards.pearl: Red flags change headache care from routine management to urgent evaluation.

flashcards.front: Before intensifying antihypertensive therapy, what should be confirmed?
flashcards.back: Confirm accurate blood pressure technique, adherence, medications/substances that elevate BP, and evidence of end-organ symptoms.
flashcards.rationale: Treatment decisions require reliable measurement and assessment for urgency or secondary contributors.
flashcards.pearl: Do not intensify chronic therapy from a single unreliable reading.

questions.stem: A patient has sudden severe headache described as maximal at onset. What is the best next step?
questions.answer: Arrange emergency evaluation for possible secondary life-threatening cause.
questions.rationale: Thunderclap headache is a red flag that requires urgent evaluation.

pearls: Primary care safety depends on recognizing when a complaint is not routine.
Red flags should drive disposition before long-term management.
